title: Count Substrings That Appear Exactly Twice
description: Count substrings appearing exactly two times

examples.explanation: String "ababa", n=5. Distinct substrings with exactly 2 occurrences: 'b' (positions 1,3), 'ab' (positions 0-1,2-3), 'ba' (positions 1-2,3-4), 'aba' (positions 0-2,2-4). Total 4.
String "aa", n=2. Distinct substrings: 'a' appears 2 times (positions 0,1), 'aa' appears 1 time. Only 'a' has exactly 2 occurrences → output 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible substrings of the input string, count their occurrences using nested loops, and tally those that appear exactly twice. This is straightforward but inefficient for large strings.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Substrings Appearing Exactly Twice Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete O(n³) solution generating all substrings and counting occurrences with nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to count substrings that appear exactly twice in a given string. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible substring of the input string, count how many times each substring appears, and increment our counter when we find a substring that appears exactly twice. The brute force approach works by using three nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index of the substring, the middle loop fixes the ending index, and the innermost loop scans through the string to count occurrences of that substring. This approach is conceptually simple but computationally expensive, making it suitable only for small strings. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize count = 0. 2. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1: a. For each ending index j from i to n-1: i. Extract substring s[i:j+1]. ii. Initialize occurrence_count = 0. iii. For each possible starting position k from 0 to n-(j-i+1): 1. If s[k:k+(j-i+1)] equals substring, increment occurrence_count. iv. If occurrence_count == 2, increment count. 3. Return count. Time complexity is O(n³) because we have three nested loops each potentially running O(n) times. Space complexity is O(n²) due to storing substrings (though we could optimize to O(1) by comparing characters directly). This connects to L1 by implementing the complete counting logic that uses the substring generation from L1. Common mistakes include: counting substrings multiple times (if we don't reset occurrence_count properly), off-by-one errors in substring indices, and inefficient string comparisons. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_substrings_twice(s): n = len(s); count = 0; for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): sub = s[i:j+1]; occ = 0; length = j-i+1; for k in range(n-length+1): if s[k:k+length] == sub: occ += 1; if occ == 2: count += 1; break; return count.

examples.trace: Distinct substrings with exactly 2 occurrences: 'b', 'ab', 'ba', 'aba'. Total 4.
Distinct substrings: 'a' appears 2 times, 'aa' appears 1 time. Only 'a' has exactly 2 occurrences → count = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Substrings and Count Occurrences for One Substring
ladders.desc: Generate all possible substrings from a string and count how many times a given substring appears.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for generating all substrings and counting occurrences of a specific substring. The intuition is that to solve the full problem, we need to examine every contiguous sequence of characters in the string, and for each such substring, determine how many times it appears elsewhere in the string. This involves two main tasks: first, iterating over all possible starting and ending indices to produce substrings; second, for a given substring, scanning the entire string to count matches. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list or set to store substrings (though in the full solution, we might process them on the fly). 2. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1: a. For each ending index j from i to n-1: i. Extract substring s[i:j+1]. ii. Count occurrences by scanning: initialize count=0, for k from 0 to n-(j-i+1): if s[k:k+length] equals substring, increment count. iii. Store or process (substring, count). 3. Return the collection of substring-count pairs. Time complexity is O(n³) for generating and counting, as the nested loops give O(n²) substrings and each count takes O(n). Space complexity is O(n²) to store all substrings. This connects to L0 by providing the essential data needed to filter for count==2. Common mistakes include: incorrect substring extraction (off-by-one), inefficient string comparison (should compare character by character to avoid O(n) slice), and not resetting count for each substring. Real code pattern: def generate_and_count(s): n=len(s); result=[]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i,n): sub=s[i:j+1]; length=j-i+1; occ=0; for k in range(n-length+1): if s[k:k+length]==sub: occ+=1; result.append((sub,occ)); return result.

examples.trace: String: aba, n=3. Generate all substrings: i=0: j=0: sub='a', count occurrences: k=0: s[0:1]='a' match → occ=1; k=1: s[1:2]='b' no; k=2: s[2:3]='a' match → occ=2. j=1: sub='ab', length=2: k=0: s[0:2]='ab' match → occ=1; k=1: s[1:3]='ba' no → occ=1. j=2: sub='aba', length=3: k=0: match → occ=1. i=1: j=1: sub='b', length=1: k=0: 'a' no; k=1: 'b' yes → occ=1; k=2: 'a' no → occ=1. j=2: sub='ba', length=2: k=0: 'ab' no; k=1: 'ba' yes → occ=1. i=2: j=2: sub='a', length=1: k=0: 'a' yes → occ=1; k=1: 'b' no; k=2: 'a' yes → occ=2. So for substring 'a', it appears in results with occ=2 at i=0 and i=2.

String: aa, n=2. Generate: i=0: j=0: sub='a', length=1: k=0: match → occ=1; k=1: match → occ=2. j=1: sub='aa', length=2: k=0: match → occ=1. i=1: j=1: sub='a', length=1: k=0: match → occ=1; k=1: match → occ=2. So for substring 'aa', it appears at i=0,j=1 with occ=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Count Occurrences of a Substring in a String
ladders.desc: Given a string and a substring, count how many times the substring appears contiguously.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on counting the occurrences of a specific substring within a larger string, which is a fundamental operation in the brute force solution. The intuition is to slide a window of length equal to the substring across the main string, comparing each window to the substring, and incrementing a counter whenever they match. This is essentially a simple string matching problem without advanced algorithms like KMP. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Let n be length of string s, m be length of substring sub. 2. If m > n, return 0. 3. Initialize count = 0. 4. For each starting index i from 0 to n-m: a. Extract window = s[i:i+m]. b. If window equals sub, increment count. 5. Return count. Time complexity is O(n*m) in worst case if we do character-by-character comparison, but using string slice might make it O(n) per comparison. In our brute force, we use slice, so it's O(n) per substring count. Space complexity is O(1) if we compare in place, or O(m) for the window. This connects to L1 by being the inner counting loop used for each generated substring. Common mistakes include: off-by-one in loop range (should be n-m inclusive), case sensitivity issues, and not handling empty substring correctly. Real code pattern: def count_occurrences(s, sub): n=len(s); m=len(sub); if m==0: return 0; count=0; for i in range(n-m+1): if s[i:i+m]==sub: count+=1; return count.

examples.trace: n=5, m=3. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0: window s[0:3]='aba' matches sub → count=1. i=1: window s[1:4]='bab' no match. i=2: window s[2:5]='aba' matches → count=2. Return 2.
n=4, m=2. Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0: s[0:2]='aa' match → count=1. i=1: s[1:3]='aa' match → count=2. i=2: s[2:4]='aa' match → count=3. Return 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generate All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Produce all possible contiguous substrings from a given string.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves generating every possible substring of a string, which is necessary for the brute force approach to examine each substring. The intuition is that a substring is defined by its starting and ending indices within the string, so by iterating over all pairs of indices, we can extract all substrings. This is a foundational concept in string manipulation problems. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Let n be the length of the string s. 2. Initialize an empty list to store substrings. 3. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1: a. For each ending index j from i to n-1: i. Extract substring s[i:j+1] and add to list. 4. Return the list. Time complexity is O(n³) if we consider the substring extraction cost, but typically O(n²) substrings are generated, each of length up to n, so total time O(n³). Space complexity is O(n²) to store all substrings. This connects to L2 by providing the substrings that need to be counted. Common mistakes include: incorrect slice indices (using j instead of j+1), generating non-contiguous sequences (which are not substrings), and not handling empty string correctly. Real code pattern: def generate_substrings(s): n=len(s); subs=[]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i,n): subs.append(s[i:j+1]); return subs.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: j=0: sub='a'; j=1: sub='ab'; j=2: sub='abc'. i=1: j=1: sub='b'; j=2: sub='bc'. i=2: j=2: sub='c'. Total 6 substrings.
n=2. i=0: j=0: 'a'; j=1: 'aa'. i=1: j=1: 'a'. Note: 'a' appears twice from different starting indices, but they are the same substring. In a set, it would be {'a','aa'}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Extract a Substring Given Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Retrieve a contiguous sequence of characters from a string using index positions.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves extracting a substring from a string, which is essential for generating and comparing substrings in the brute force solution. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters indexed from 0, and a substring is a slice of this sequence defined by a starting index (inclusive) and an ending index (exclusive in many languages, but we'll use inclusive for clarity). This operation allows us to isolate specific parts of the string for further processing. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Given string s, start index i, and end index j (with 0 ≤ i ≤ j < len(s)). 2. Initialize an empty string result. 3. For each index k from i to j: a. Append character s[k] to result. 4. Return result. Alternatively, use built-in slice operation s[i:j+1]. Time complexity is O(length of substring) for manual extraction, or O(1) amortized for slice. Space complexity is O(length) to store the substring. This connects to L3 by being the method used to generate each substring from the indices. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., using j instead of j+1 for exclusive slice), indices out of bounds, and not handling empty substring (when i>j). Real code pattern: def extract_substring(s, i, j): return s[i:j+1].

examples.trace: String: hello, indices: 0=h,1=e,2=l,3=l,4=o. Extract from i=1 to j=3 inclusive: characters at positions 1,2,3 are e,l,l → substring 'ell'.
String: abc, indices: 0=a,1=b,2=c. Extract from 0 to 2 inclusive: a,b,c → 'abc'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Strings and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what strings are and how to access individual characters using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces strings as sequences of characters and indexing as a way to access specific positions within a string. The intuition is that in programming, a string is like an array of characters, where each character has a position number starting from 0. Understanding this is crucial for manipulating substrings, as substrings are defined by ranges of these indices. The step-by-step explanation: 1. A string is a data type that represents text, e.g., "hello". 2. Each character in the string has an index: for "hello", index 0 is 'h', 1 is 'e', 2 is 'l', 3 is 'l', 4 is 'o'. 3. To access a character, use square brackets: s[i] returns the character at index i. 4. Indices must be within bounds: 0 ≤ i < length of string. 5. The length of a string is the number of characters, obtained via len(s). This concept connects to L4 by providing the basic knowledge needed to extract substrings using indices. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (thinking first character is at index 1), accessing out-of-bounds indices, and confusing strings with lists. Real code pattern: s = "example"; char = s[0]  # 'e'; length = len(s)  # 7.

examples.trace: String 'cat' has indices: 0='c', 1='a', 2='t'. Accessing index 1 gives character 'a'.

examples.input: String: "123", find length and access last index
examples.trace: String '123' has indices 0='1',1='2',2='3'. Length is 3. Last index is 2, so s[2] gives '3'.

approaches.name: HashMap O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store counts of all substrings generated by nested loops, then iterate over the map to count those with exactly two occurrences. This reduces time by avoiding repeated substring comparisons.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Substrings Appearing Exactly Twice Using HashMap O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all substrings that appear exactly two times in a string using nested loops and a hashmap.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires counting all distinct substrings that appear exactly twice in a given string. The brute-force approach would involve generating all substrings and comparing each with every other, leading to O(n⁴) time complexity. The HashMap O(n²) approach optimizes this by using a hashmap to store counts of all substrings generated through nested loops, then iterating over the map to count those with exactly two occurrences. This reduces time complexity to O(n²) by avoiding repeated substring comparisons, though space complexity remains O(n²) due to storing all substrings.

Intuition: The key insight is that we can generate all possible substrings efficiently using two nested loops (outer loop for start index, inner loop for end index). By storing each substring in a hashmap with its count, we avoid recomputing comparisons. After populating the map, we simply count entries with value 2.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty hashmap `count_map`.
2. For each start index `i` from 0 to n-1:
   a. For each end index `j` from i to n-1:
      i. Extract substring s[i:j+1].
      ii. Increment its count in `count_map`.
3. Initialize `result = 0`.
4. For each value in `count_map.values()`:
   a. If value == 2, increment `result`.
5. Return `result`.

Time complexity: O(n²) for generating substrings (n choices for start, up to n for end). Space complexity: O(n²) as there are O(n²) substrings stored.

Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of populating the hashmap with substring counts, which is detailed in L1.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to include single-character substrings, off-by-one errors in substring extraction, or misunderstanding that we count distinct substrings (not occurrences).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def count_substrings_twice(s):
    count_map = {}
    n = len(s)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i, n):
            sub = s[i:j+1]
            count_map[sub] = count_map.get(sub, 0) + 1
    result = 0
    for count in count_map.values():
        if count == 2:
            result += 1
    return result

examples.trace:

- Length 3: 'aba' (0-2,2-4) → count=2, 'bab' (1-3) → count=1, 'aba' (2-4) already counted
- Length 4: 'abab' (0-3) → count=1, 'baba' (1-4) → count=1
- Length 5: 'ababa' (0-4) → count=1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Populate HashMap with Substring Counts Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all substrings of a string and store their frequencies in a hashmap.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the core of the full solution, responsible for efficiently generating all possible substrings and counting their occurrences using a hashmap. The intuition is that by iterating over all start and end indices, we can extract every substring without redundancy, and the hashmap allows O(1) average-time updates to counts, avoiding the need for repeated searches or comparisons.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty hashmap `count_map`.
2. Let `n` be the length of the input string `s`.
3. For `i` from 0 to n-1 (start index):
   a. For `j` from i to n-1 (end index):
      i. Extract substring `sub = s[i:j+1]`.
      ii. If `sub` is in `count_map`, increment its count by 1; else, set count to 1.
4. Return `count_map`.

Time complexity: O(n²) because there are O(n²) substrings (n choices for start, up to n for end), and each substring operation and hashmap update is O(1) on average. Space complexity: O(n²) to store up to n² substrings in the hashmap.

Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the hashmap needed in the full problem to count substrings with exactly two occurrences.

Common mistakes: Using inefficient substring extraction that copies the string repeatedly (in some languages), incorrect loop bounds leading to missing substrings, or not handling empty strings properly.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def build_substring_count_map(s):
    count_map = {}
    n = len(s)
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i, n):
            sub = s[i:j+1]
            count_map[sub] = count_map.get(sub, 0) + 1
    return count_map

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Over All Substrings Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Generate all possible substrings of a string by iterating through start and end indices.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves using two nested loops to systematically generate every substring of a given string. The intuition is that any substring can be uniquely identified by its start index (i) and end index (j), where i ≤ j. By iterating i from 0 to n-1 and j from i to n-1, we cover all possible substrings without repetition.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Let `s` be the input string of length `n`.
2. For `i` from 0 to n-1:
   a. For `j` from i to n-1:
      i. The substring is `s[i:j+1]`.

Time complexity: O(n²) as there are n(n+1)/2 substrings, which is O(n²). Space complexity: O(1) extra space, but if storing substrings, it becomes O(n²).

Connection to L1: This logic is used in L1 to generate substrings for populating the hashmap.

Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in substring extraction (e.g., using s[i:j] instead of s[i:j+1]), or misunderstanding that j starts from i (not 0).

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def generate_all_substrings(s):
    n = len(s)
    substrings = []
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i, n):
            substrings.append(s[i:j+1])
    return substrings

examples.trace:
String: "abc", n=3
i=0: j=0: sub='a', j=1: sub='ab', j=2: sub='abc'
i=1: j=1: sub='b', j=2: sub='bc'
i=2: j=2: sub='c'
List: ['a','ab','abc','b','bc','c']

String: "aa", n=2
i=0: j=0: sub='a', j=1: sub='aa'
i=1: j=1: sub='a'
List: ['a','aa','a']

ladders.title: Building Block — Extract Substrings by Index Ranges
ladders.desc: Given a string and start/end indices, extract the substring between them.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on extracting a substring from a string using start and end indices. The intuition is that a substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string, defined by a start index (inclusive) and an end index (inclusive). In most programming languages, this is done using slicing or substring methods.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: string `s`, start index `i`, end index `j` (with 0 ≤ i ≤ j < length of s).
2. Return the substring consisting of characters from index i to index j, inclusive.

Time complexity: O(k) where k is the length of the substring (j-i+1), as it may involve copying characters. Space complexity: O(k) for the new substring.

Connection to L2: This operation is used repeatedly in L2 to get each substring when iterating over indices.

Common mistakes: Confusing zero-based indexing, using exclusive end indices incorrectly, or not validating that i ≤ j.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def extract_substring(s, i, j):
    return s[i:j+1]

examples.trace:
String: "hello", indices: i=1, j=3
Characters: index 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'
Substring from i=1 to j=3 inclusive: 'e','l','l' → "ell"

String: "algorithm", indices: i=2, j=5
Characters: 0='a',1='l',2='g',3='o',4='r',5='i',6='t',7='h',8='m'
Substring from i=2 to j=5: 'g','o','r','i' → "gori"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over String Indices with a Loop
ladders.desc: Use a loop to access each character or index in a string sequentially.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves iterating over the indices of a string using a loop, typically a for-loop. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access each character by its index, starting from 0 up to length-1. This is fundamental for processing strings character by character or in chunks.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Let `s` be a string of length `n`.
2. For `i` from 0 to n-1:
   a. Access character `s[i]` or perform operations using index `i`.

Time complexity: O(n) for iterating through n indices. Space complexity: O(1) extra space.

Connection to L3: This operation is used in L3 to define the start and end indices for substring extraction.

Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing, going out of bounds (i.e., accessing index n), or not handling empty strings.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def iterate_string_indices(s):
    n = len(s)
    for i in range(n):
        # Do something with s[i] or i

examples.trace:
String: "cat", length n=3
Loop i from 0 to 2:
i=0: character 'c'
i=1: character 'a'
i=2: character 't'
Indices accessed: 0,1,2

String: "", length n=0
Loop i from 0 to -1: no iteration
No indices accessed.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String and How to Access Its Length?
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences of characters and how to get their length.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept introduces strings, which are fundamental data types in programming representing sequences of characters. A string can be empty ("") or contain one or more characters like letters, digits, or symbols. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains, and it can be accessed using a built-in function or property, such as `len()` in Python.

Intuition: Think of a string as a list of characters where each character has a position starting from 0. Knowing the length helps in iterating or manipulating the string without going out of bounds.

Step-by-step:
1. Define a string, e.g., `s = "hello"`.
2. Get its length: `n = len(s)` gives 5.
3. Characters: s[0]='h', s[1]='e', s[2]='l', s[3]='l', s[4]='o'.

Time complexity: O(1) to get length in most languages. Space complexity: O(n) to store the string.

Connection to L4: This concept is prerequisite for L4, where we use the length to iterate over indices.

Common mistakes: Confusing string length with last index (length-1), or not handling empty strings properly.

Real code pattern (Python-style):
s = "example"
n = len(s)  # n is 7

examples.trace:
String: "apple"
Characters: 'a','p','p','l','e'
Count characters: 5
Length = 5

String: "" (empty string)
No characters
Length = 0

approaches.name: Rolling Hash O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Employ rolling hash (e.g., Rabin-Karp) to compute hashes for all substrings in O(n^2) time, store counts in a hashmap, and filter for exactly two occurrences. This optimizes substring generation with hashing.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Substrings Appearing Exactly Twice Using Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Complete solution using rolling hash to count substrings with exactly two occurrences in O(n²) time.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all substrings of a given string that appear exactly twice. A naive approach would generate all O(n²) substrings and compare them directly, leading to O(n³) time. The rolling hash approach optimizes this by using polynomial hashing to compute substring hashes in O(1) time after preprocessing, reducing the overall complexity to O(n²). The intuition is that we can represent each substring as a hash value, allowing us to quickly count occurrences using a hashmap. We generate all substrings, compute their hashes using rolling hash, store counts in a hashmap, and finally count those with exactly two occurrences. The algorithm involves: 1) Precomputing powers of a base modulo a prime for O(1) hash computation. 2) Precomputing prefix hashes for the string. 3) Iterating over all start and end indices to compute substring hashes via rolling hash formula: hash(s[l:r]) = (prefix_hash[r] - prefix_hash[l] * pow_base[r-l]) % mod. 4) Storing hash counts in a hashmap. 5) Filtering hashes with count exactly 2. Time complexity is O(n²) for generating all substrings and computing hashes, with O(1) hash computation per substring. Space complexity is O(n²) in worst case for storing all substring hashes, but can be optimized to O(n) by not storing all hashes explicitly. Connection to L1: This level integrates the rolling hash subroutine from L1 into the full solution. Common mistakes include hash collisions (mitigated by using a large prime modulus and possibly double hashing), integer overflow (use modulo operations carefully), and off-by-one errors in substring indices. Real code pattern: precompute powers and prefix hashes, nested loops for start and end, compute hash, update countmap, iterate over countmap to count exactly twos.

examples.trace: String: 'ababa', n=5. Precompute: base=31, mod=10^9+7, powers=[1,31,961,...], prefix_hashes=[0,97,3108,...]. Generate substrings: length 1: 'a'(hash=97) count=3, 'b'(hash=98) count=2 → exactly twice? 'b' yes. length 2: 'ab'(hash=3108) count=2 → yes, 'ba'(hash=3035) count=2 → yes, 'ab'(hash=3108) again, 'ba'(hash=3035) again. length 3: 'aba'(hash=97031) count=2 → yes, 'bab'(hash=94178) count=1, 'aba'(hash=97031) again. length 4: 'abab'(hash=3009998) count=1, 'baba'(hash=2929995) count=1. length 5: 'ababa'(hash=93309989) count=1. Count exactly twice: substrings 'b', 'ab', 'ba', 'aba' → 4.

String: 'aaaa', n=4. Precompute as above. Substrings: length 1: 'a' count=4. length 2: 'aa' count=3. length 3: 'aaa' count=2 → exactly twice? yes. length 4: 'aaaa' count=1. Also, note 'aa' appears 3 times (positions 0-1,1-2,2-3), not exactly twice. 'a' appears 4 times. Only 'aaa' appears exactly twice (positions 0-2 and 1-3). Wait, check: substrings exactly twice? 'aaa' appears twice, also 'aa'? count=3, no. 'a'? count=4, no. 'aaaa'? count=1, no. So only 'aaa'. But output is 3? Let's list all substrings: 'a','a','a','a' (4 times), 'aa','aa','aa' (3 times), 'aaa','aaa' (2 times), 'aaaa' (1 time). Exactly twice: 'aaa' only → 1. But problem might count distinct substrings? Actually, problem counts substrings (not distinct) that appear exactly twice. So 'aaa' appears twice, count 1 substring. But example says 3? Re-check: For 'aaaa', substrings: indices (0,0),(1,1),(2,2),(3,3) all 'a' → count 4. (0,1),(1,2),(2,3) all 'aa' → count 3. (0,2),(1,3) all 'aaa' → count 2. (0,3) 'aaaa' → count 1. So exactly twice: only 'aaa' → 1. But given output 3, maybe I misinterpret. Actually, problem: 'Count substrings appearing exactly two times' means count all substrings (by their content) that appear exactly twice in the string. So for 'aaaa', substring 'a' appears 4 times, 'aa' appears 3 times, 'aaa' appears 2 times, 'aaaa' appears 1 time. So only 'aaa' qualifies → count 1. But to match common test, output is 3? Let's compute carefully: Using rolling hash, we compute hash for each substring: hashes for 'aaa' at (0,2) and (1,3) are same, count=2 → exactly twice. Others have count not 2. So result 1. But in ladder L0, I'll use correct example. Let's use 'ababa' output 4 as first example, and for second, use 'abcabc' output 9. Trace for 'abcabc': n=6. Substrings exactly twice: 'a','b','c','ab','bc','ca','abc','bca','cab' → 9. Use rolling hash to compute counts.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rolling Hash Computation for All Substrings
ladders.desc: Compute hashes for all substrings of a string using rolling hash in O(n²) time.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution, responsible for efficiently generating hashes for every possible substring. The intuition is that by precomputing prefix hashes and powers of a base modulo a prime, we can compute the hash of any substring in O(1) time using the formula: hash(s[l:r]) = (prefix_hash[r] - prefix_hash[l] * power[r-l]) % mod, where prefix_hash[i] is the hash of prefix s[0:i], and power[i] is base^i mod mod. This avoids recomputing hashes from scratch for each substring, saving time from O(n) per substring to O(1). The algorithm steps: 1) Choose a base (e.g., 31) and a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9+7). 2) Precompute an array power where power[i] = base^i % mod for i from 0 to n. 3) Precompute prefix_hash array where prefix_hash[0]=0, and for i from 1 to n, prefix_hash[i] = (prefix_hash[i-1] * base + ord(s[i-1])) % mod. 4) For each start index l from 0 to n-1, and each end index r from l+1 to n, compute hash = (prefix_hash[r] - prefix_hash[l] * power[r-l]) % mod, adjust to positive if negative. This yields all substring hashes. Time complexity: O(n²) for the double loop, with O(1) hash computation per substring. Space complexity: O(n) for precomputed arrays. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the hashes needed for counting in the full problem. Common mistakes: forgetting to mod operations, integer overflow (use long integers), incorrect indexing (ensure r is exclusive). Real code pattern: precompute power and prefix_hash, then nested loops to compute and store hashes.

examples.trace: String: 'abc', n=3. base=31, mod=1000000007. Precompute power: power[0]=1, power[1]=31, power[2]=961, power[3]=29791. Precompute prefix_hash: prefix_hash[0]=0, prefix_hash[1]=ord('a')=97, prefix_hash[2]=(97*31+98)%mod=3105, prefix_hash[3]=(3105*31+99)%mod=96354. Now compute substring hashes: l=0,r=1: hash=(prefix_hash[1]-prefix_hash[0]*power[1])%mod=(97-0)%mod=97 for 'a'. l=0,r=2: hash=(prefix_hash[2]-prefix_hash[0]*power[2])%mod=(3105-0)%mod=3105 for 'ab'. l=0,r=3: hash=(prefix_hash[3]-prefix_hash[0]*power[3])%mod=(96354-0)%mod=96354 for 'abc'. l=1,r=2: hash=(prefix_hash[2]-prefix_hash[1]*power[1])%mod=(3105-97*31)%mod=(3105-3007)%mod=98 for 'b'. l=1,r=3: hash=(prefix_hash[3]-prefix_hash[1]*power[2])%mod=(96354-97*961)%mod=(96354-93217)%mod=3137 for 'bc'. l=2,r=3: hash=(prefix_hash[3]-prefix_hash[2]*power[1])%mod=(96354-3105*31)%mod=(96354-96255)%mod=99 for 'c'. So hashes: 97,3105,96354,98,3137,99.

String: 'aa', n=2. base=31, mod=1000000007. power: [1,31,961]. prefix_hash: [0,97,3105]. Substrings: l=0,r=1: hash=(97-0*31)%mod=97 for 'a'. l=0,r=2: hash=(3105-0*961)%mod=3105 for 'aa'. l=1,r=2: hash=(3105-97*31)%mod=(3105-3007)%mod=98 for 'a'. Note: both 'a' substrings have hash 97? Wait, compute: for l=1,r=2, hash=(prefix_hash[2]-prefix_hash[1]*power[1])%mod=(3105-97*31)%mod=(3105-3007)=98. But 'a' should have same hash? Actually, hash depends on position? No, rolling hash for same substring should be same. Here, 'a' at position 0 and 1 are same string 'a', but hash computed: for l=0,r=1: 97, for l=1,r=2: 98. This is incorrect because formula might need adjustment. Actually, prefix_hash[i] is hash for s[0:i], so for substring s[l:r], hash = (prefix_hash[r] - prefix_hash[l] * power[r-l]) % mod. For 'a' at l=1,r=2: prefix_hash[2]=hash('aa')= (ord('a')*base + ord('a'))%mod = (97*31+97)%mod=3105? Let's compute: 97*31=3007, +97=3104, mod 1000000007=3104. But I used 3105 earlier, error. Recompute properly: prefix_hash[1] = 97. prefix_hash[2] = (97*31 + 97)%mod = (3007+97)%mod=3104. Then for l=1,r=2: hash = (3104 - 97*31)%mod = (3104-3007)=97. Yes, so both 'a' have hash 97. Correct trace: power[0]=1, power[1]=31, power[2]=961. prefix_hash[0]=0, prefix_hash[1]=97, prefix_hash[2]=3104. l=0,r=1: hash=97. l=0,r=2: hash=3104. l=1,r=2: hash=97.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Polynomial Rolling Hash Formula
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the rolling hash formula to compute substring hashes in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the mathematical formula that enables O(1) hash computation for any substring given precomputed prefix hashes and powers. The intuition is to treat the string as a polynomial in a base B, where each character is a digit, and the hash is the polynomial value modulo a prime P. For a string s, the hash of prefix up to index i is H(i) = (s[0]*B^(i-1) + s[1]*B^(i-2) + ... + s[i-1]*B^0) mod P. Then, the hash of substring s[l:r] is H(r) - H(l)*B^(r-l) mod P. This works because H(r) includes terms from s[0] to s[r-1], and subtracting H(l)*B^(r-l) removes terms before index l, scaled appropriately. Step-by-step: 1) Define base B and modulus P. 2) Precompute array pow where pow[i] = B^i mod P. 3) Precompute prefix hash array H where H[0]=0, H[i] = (H[i-1]*B + s[i-1]) mod P for i>0. 4) For substring from l to r, hash = (H[r] - H[l]*pow[r-l]) mod P, ensure non-negative. Time per substring: O(1). Space: O(n) for arrays. Connection to L1: This formula is used in the subroutine to compute all substring hashes. Common mistakes: using small base/modulus causing collisions, not handling negative results after modulo, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: hash = (prefix[r] - prefix[l] * power[r-l] % mod + mod) % mod.

examples.trace: Given s='ab', B=31, P=1000000007. Precompute: pow[0]=1, pow[1]=31, pow[2]=961. H[0]=0, H[1]=97, H[2]=(97*31+98)%P=3105. For substring s[0:2] (indices 0 to 2 exclusive), l=0, r=2. hash = (H[2] - H[0]*pow[2])%P = (3105 - 0*961)%P = 3105. Verify: 'ab' has characters 'a'(97) and 'b'(98). Direct hash: (97*31^1 + 98*31^0) mod P = (97*31 + 98) mod P = (3007+98)=3105 mod P. Match.

ladders.title: Building Block — Precomputing Prefix Hashes and Powers
ladders.desc: Compute prefix hashes and powers of base modulo prime for a string.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves precomputing two arrays: prefix hashes and powers of the base modulo a prime, which are essential for the rolling hash formula. The intuition is that by computing these arrays once in O(n) time, we can answer any substring hash query in O(1) time later. Prefix hash H[i] represents the hash of the substring s[0:i] (first i characters). It is computed iteratively: H[0] = 0, and for i from 1 to n, H[i] = (H[i-1] * base + ord(s[i-1])) % mod. This builds the polynomial hash incrementally. Powers array pow[i] stores base^i % mod, needed to scale hashes when computing substring hashes. It is computed as pow[0] = 1, and for i from 1 to n, pow[i] = (pow[i-1] * base) % mod. These precomputations require O(n) time and O(n) space. Connection to L2: These arrays are used in the rolling hash formula to compute substring hashes. Common mistakes: using wrong initial values (H[0] must be 0 for formula to work), integer overflow during multiplication (use modulo at each step), forgetting to compute up to length n. Real code pattern: initialize arrays, loop to compute using modular arithmetic.

testCases.expected: powers up to 4, prefixes up to 4
powers up to 5, prefixes up to 5
powers up to 6, prefixes up to 6
powers up to 11, prefixes up to 11

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic for Hashing
ladders.desc: Perform modular addition, multiplication, and subtraction to compute hashes.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using modular arithmetic to compute hash values while avoiding integer overflow and ensuring results are within a fixed range. The intuition is that hashing often uses large numbers, so we perform operations modulo a prime number to keep numbers manageable and reduce collisions. Key operations: 1) Modular addition: (a + b) % mod. 2) Modular multiplication: (a * b) % mod. 3) Modular subtraction: (a - b) % mod, which may yield negative results; we adjust by adding mod if negative to get a positive remainder. In rolling hash, we use these to compute prefix hashes and substring hashes. For example, prefix hash: new_hash = (old_hash * base + char_code) % mod. For substring hash: hash = (prefix_hash[r] - prefix_hash[l] * power) % mod, with adjustment. Time per operation is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used in precomputing prefix hashes and powers. Common mistakes: forgetting to mod after each operation, leading to overflow; incorrect handling of negative values in subtraction; using mod that is not prime, increasing collision chance. Real code pattern: use % operator after each arithmetic step, and for subtraction: (a - b + mod) % mod.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Substrings and Hashing Basics
ladders.desc: Understand what substrings are and the basic idea of hashing for string comparison.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces two key concepts: substrings and hashing. A substring of a string is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string. For a string of length n, there are n*(n+1)/2 possible substrings (including empty string, but typically we consider non-empty). For example, string 'abc' has substrings: 'a', 'b', 'c', 'ab', 'bc', 'abc'. Hashing is a technique to map data (like strings) to fixed-size values (hashes) for efficient comparison. The idea is that if two strings are equal, their hashes are equal (but not vice-versa due to collisions). We use a hash function that computes a number from the string's characters. For rolling hash, we use a polynomial hash: hash(s) = (s[0]*B^(k-1) + s[1]*B^(k-2) + ... + s[k-1]*B^0) mod P, where B is a base, P is a prime modulus, and k is string length. This allows efficient computation when we extend or shrink the string. Connection to L4: Hashing uses modular arithmetic as in L4. Common mistakes: confusing substrings with subsequences (which need not be contiguous), not understanding that hashes can collide. Real code pattern: to get all substrings, use nested loops for start and end indices.

examples.trace: String 'ab' has length 2. Substrings: start at index 0, length 1: 'a'. start at index 0, length 2: 'ab'. start at index 1, length 1: 'b'. So total 3 substrings: 'a', 'ab', 'b'.

examples.input: Compute hash for 'hi' with B=31, P=1009

testCases.expected: Substrings: x, y, z, xy, yz, xyz
